blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١)
When the Imminent [Hour] befalls (1)
لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِهَا كَاذِبَةٌ (٢)
—there is no denying that it will befall— (2)
خَافِضَةٌ رَّافِعَةٌ (٣)
[it will be] lowering and exalting. (3)
إِذَا رُجَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ رَجًّا (٤)
When the earth is shaken violently, (4)
وَبُسَّتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ بَسًّا (٥)
and the mountains are shattered into bits (5)
فَكَانَتْ هَبَآءً مُّنۢبَثًّا (٦)
and become scattered dust, (6)
وَكُنتُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا ثَلَـٰثَةً (٧)
you will be three groups: (7)
فَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (٨)
The People of the Right Hand—and what are the People of the Right Hand?! (8)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ (٩)
And the People of the Left Hand—and what are the People of the Left Hand?! (9)
وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ (١٠)
And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: (10)

(Commentary)

The Surah describes the Great Resurrection (القيامة الكبرى) — the final and universal day of rising when all humanity is brought back to life for judgment — in which the people are resurrected, held to account, and recompensed. It first mentions some of its terrors that closely affect man and the earth he inhabits, noting the overturning of conditions and states through abasement and elevation, the violent shaking of the earth, and the crumbling of the mountains. It categorizes people into three groups (أزواج) — distinct classes or types of beings — in general terms, and then details the final state of each: the Foremost (السابقون), the People of the Right (أصحاب اليمين), and the People of the Left (أصحاب الشمال).

Thereafter, it presents arguments against the People of the Left who deny His Lordship (الربوبية) and the Resurrection (البعث), and who reject the Quran that calls toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and faith in the Resurrection. The Surah concludes by mentioning the moment of death (الاحتضار) — the state of the soul as it prepares to leave the body — with the descent of death and the division of people into these three groups.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ "When the Event befalls" (1). The "befalling" (وقوع) of an incident is its occurrence. "The Event" (الواقعة) is an attribute used to describe any incident; however, what is intended here is the Event of the Resurrection. It has been used as a proper noun (إطلاق الأعلام), as if it requires no estimated noun to qualify, which is why it is said to be among the names of the Resurrection in the Quran, like "the Inevitable" (الحاقة), "the Rattler" (القارعة), and "the Overwhelmer" (الغاشية).

The phrase إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ carries the meaning of a conditional clause, yet the apodosis (جزاء الشرط) — the result or consequence of the condition — is omitted to magnify its gravity and emphasize its significance. In any case, it is understood from what the Surah will describe regarding the state of people on the Day of Resurrection; the estimation would be something like: "the believers shall triumph and the disbelievers shall lose."

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ "there is no denying its happening" (2). It is stated in al-Majma‘ that "denying/lie" (الكاذبة) is a verbal noun (مصدر) like "well-being" (العافية) and "outcome" (العاقبة). Accordingly, the meaning is: there is no falsehood (كذب) in its occurrence or its realization. It is also said that "denying" (كاذبة) is an adjective whose qualified noun is omitted, the estimation being: "there is no false soul/claim (قضية كاذبة) regarding its occurrence."

His saying, the Exalted: خافِضَةٌ رافِعَةٌ "abasing [some], exalting [others]" (3). These are two predicates whose subject is the pronoun referring back to "the Event." Abasing (الخفض) is the opposite of exalting (الرفع). Its being "abasing and exalting" is an allusion (كناية) to its overturning of the observed system of the world; thus, the inner secrets (السرائر) will manifest while they are hidden today, and the effects and links of secondary causes (الأسباب) will be veiled and concealed while they are apparent today. It shall humiliate the "mighty" among the folk of disbelief and wickedness, and exalt the God-conscious (المتقين).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا "when the earth is shaken with a violent shaking" (4). Shaking (الرج) is to move something with great intensity, referring to the earthquake of the Hour (زلزلة الساعة) which Allah, the Glorified, magnifies in His saying: إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْءٌ عَظِيمٌ "Indeed the quaking of the Hour is a terrible thing" (22:1). He has magnified it in this verse by expressing it as the "shaking of the earth," then emphasized its intensity by the indefiniteness of His saying رَجًّا (a shaking), meaning a shaking whose intensity cannot be described. This sentence serves as a substitute (بدل) or an explanation (بيان) for His saying: إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا "and the mountains are crumbled into powder, and become scattered dust" (5-6). This is coordinated (عطف) with "shaken" (رُجَّت). Crumbling (البس) is to crush (الفت); it is the reduction of a body into tiny, vanishing parts like flour through pounding or the like. It is also said that al-bass means to set in motion, thus carrying the meaning of His saying: وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ "and the mountains will be set in motion" (78:20).

His saying: فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا — Dust (الهباء) is said to be fine dust (الغبار), or the motes of dust visible in a ray of sunlight entering through an aperture. Scattering (الانبثاث) is dispersion (التفرق). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً "and you are three groups" (7). Group (الزوج) here means category or class (الصنف), and the address is to all of humanity.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ "As for the People of the Right Hand, who are the People of the Right Hand?" (8). This branches off from what preceded it as an explanation (بيان) branches from the thing explained (المبين). This verse and the two following it are an explanation of the three groups.

The "Right Hand" (الميمنة) is derived from "auspiciousness" (اليمن), which is the opposite of "ill-fortune" (الشؤم). Thus, the People of the Right Hand are the people of felicity (السعادة) and auspiciousness, in contrast to the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب المشأمة) — the people of wretchedness (الشقاء) and ill-fortune. The view that "Right Hand" (الميمنة) refers to the right side (اليمين) because they are given their records in their right hands while others are given them in their left is refuted by the juxtaposition of the People of the Right Hand with the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب المشأمة). Had it been as suggested, it would have said "the People of the Left" (أصحاب الشمال), as is apparent.

The word "what" (ما) in His saying ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ is interrogative (استفهامية) and serves as a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ. The entire phrase then serves as the predicate for the initial فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ. The interrogation serves to magnify their matter and exalt their status.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ "And the People of the Left Hand, who are the People of the Left Hand?" (9). The "Left Hand" (المشأمة) is a verbal noun like "ill-fortune" (الشؤم), the opposite of the right. Thus, the "Right Hand" (الميمنة) and "Left Hand" (المشأمة) signify felicity and wretchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ "And the Foremost Ones are the Foremost Ones" (10). That which is suitable to interpret the first "Foremost" (السابقون) is His saying, the Exalted: فَمِنْهُمْ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سابِقٌ بِالْخَيْراتِ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "Then some of them are wrongdoers to themselves, and some of them are average, and some of them are foremost in good deeds by Allah’s will" (35:32); and His saying: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so hasten to take the lead in all good works" (2:148); and His saying: أُولئِكَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَهُمْ لَها سابِقُونَ "it is they who hasten to good deeds, and they are the foremost in them" (23:61).

Thus, what is intended by the first "Foremost" in the verse are those who are foremost in righteous deeds (الخيرات). When they are foremost in good deeds, they are foremost in reaching the forgiveness and mercy that correspond to them, as He, the Exalted, said: سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ "Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and at Paradise" (57:21). So, those foremost in good deeds are those foremost in mercy, which is His saying: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ.

It is also said that the second "Foremost" is the same as the first, similar to the poetic expression: "I am Abu al-Najm, and my poetry is my poetry."

In the phrase وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ, the first is the subject and the second is the predicate. It is also said that the first is the subject and the second is an emphasis (تأكيد), while the predicate is His saying: أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ "They are the ones brought near" (11).

There are other opinions regarding the interpretation of "the Foremost": it is said they are those who hasten to everything Allah has called toward; it is said they are those who preceded in faith and obedience without hesitation; it is said they are the Prophets (عليه السلام) because they are the leaders of the followers of religions; it is said they are the Believer of the People of Pharaoh, Habib al-Najjar mentioned in Surah Ya-Sin, and Ali (عليه السلام) who was the first to believe in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and is the most excellent of them; it is said they are those who were foremost in the Migration (الهجرة); it is said they are those who are foremost in the five daily prayers; it is said they are those who prayed toward both Qiblahs; it is said they are those foremost in Jihad; and other things have been said.

The first two opinions return to the meaning previously mentioned. The third and fourth should be understood as examples (التمثيل), and the rest are as you see, unless they are also taken as types of examples.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Khisal, narrated from al-Zuhri, who said: I heard Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام) saying: "Whoever does not find solace in the solace of Allah, his soul will be torn apart by regrets over the world. By Allah, the world and the Hereafter are but like the two pans of a scale—whichever one outweighs the other takes the other away." Then he recited the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ — meaning the Resurrection — لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ خافِضَةٌ — "By Allah, it abases the enemies of Allah into the Fire," رافِعَةٌ — "By Allah, it exalts the friends of Allah to Paradise."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ, he said: "The Resurrection is a truth." Regarding His saying خافِضَةٌ, he said: "With the enemies of Allah," رافِعَةٌ — "For the friends of Allah." Regarding إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا, he said: "Parts of it will crash against others." Regarding وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا, he said: "The mountains will be uprooted entirely." Regarding فَكانَت هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا, he said: "The dust (الهباء) is that which is in the aperture from the sun's rays."

Regarding His saying وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً, he said: "On the Day of Resurrection," فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ وَأَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ — "Those who were foremost to Paradise."

I say: His saying "those who were foremost to Paradise" is an interpretation of the second "Foremost."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) that he said: "The scattered dust (الهباء المنبث) is the stirred-up motes (رهج الذرات), and the dispersed dust (الهباء المنثور) is the sun-dust that you see in the rays from an aperture."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ: "It was revealed concerning Ezekiel, the Believer of the People of Pharaoh; Habib al-Najjar who is mentioned in Ya-Sin; and Ali ibn Abi Talib. Each of these men was the foremost of his nation, and Ali is the most excellent of them in precedence."

In al-Majma‘, narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The Foremost are four: the son of Adam who was killed; the foremost of the nation of Moses, who was the Believer of the People of Pharaoh; the foremost of the nation of Jesus, who was Habib; and the foremost in the nation of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in Rawdat al-Wa‘izin from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

In Amali al-Shaykh, with its chain to Ibn Abbas, who said: I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ. He said: "Gabriel said to me: That is Ali and his followers (شيعته); they are the foremost to Paradise, brought near to Allah by His honoring of them."

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain to Khaythamah al-Ju‘fi from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "And we are the Foremost (السابقون السابقون) and we are the Last."

In al-Uyun, in the chapter on what has been narrated from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام) of collected reports, with its chain to Ali (عليه السلام), who said: "وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ was revealed concerning me."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the verse: "It is said: [Foremost] to the five daily prayers." Narrated from Ali (عليه السلام).

I say: The correct approach is to treat all these reports as examples (التمثيل), as previously mentioned.

Ayahs

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (١١)
they are the ones brought near [to Allah], (11)
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (١٢)
[who will reside] in the gardens of bliss. (12)
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
A multitude from the former [generations] (13)
وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٤)
and a few from the latter ones. (14)
عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّوْضُونَةٍ (١٥)
On brocaded couches (15)
مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَيْهَا مُتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (١٦)
reclining on them, face to face. (16)
يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدَٰنٌ مُّخَلَّدُونَ (١٧)
They will be waited upon by immortal youths, (17)
بِأَكْوَابٍ وَأَبَارِيقَ وَكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍ (١٨)
with goblets and ewers and a cup of a clear wine, (18)
لَّا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْهَا وَلَا يُنزِفُونَ (١٩)
which causes them neither headache nor stupefaction, (19)
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ مِّمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ (٢٠)
and such fruits as they prefer (20)
وَلَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٢١)
and such flesh of fowls as they desire, (21)
وَحُورٌ عِينٌ (٢٢)
and big-eyed houris (22)
كَأَمْثَـٰلِ ٱللُّؤْلُؤِ ٱلْمَكْنُونِ (٢٣)
like guarded pearls, (23)
جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٤)
a reward for what they used to do. (24)
لَا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا تَأْثِيمًا (٢٥)
They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, (25)
إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلَـٰمًا سَلَـٰمًا (٢٦)
but only the watchword, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ (26)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٢٧)
And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?! (27)
فِى سِدْرٍ مَّخْضُودٍ (٢٨)
Amid thornless lote trees (28)
وَطَلْحٍ مَّنضُودٍ (٢٩)
and clustered spathes (29)
وَظِلٍّ مَّمْدُودٍ (٣٠)
and extended shade, (30)
وَمَآءٍ مَّسْكُوبٍ (٣١)
and ever-flowing water (31)
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ (٣٢)
and abundant fruits, (32)
لَّا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَلَا مَمْنُوعَةٍ (٣٣)
neither inaccessible, nor forbidden, (33)
وَفُرُشٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ (٣٤)
and noble spouses. (34)
إِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُنَّ إِنشَآءً (٣٥)
We have created them with a special creation, (35)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُنَّ أَبْكَارًا (٣٦)
and made them virgins, (36)
عُرُبًا أَتْرَابًا (٣٧)
loving, of a like age, (37)
لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٨)
for the People of the Right Hand. (38)
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٩)
A multitude from the former [generations] (39)
وَثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٠)
and a multitude from the latter [ones]. (40)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ (٤١)
And the People of the Left Hand—what are the People of the Left Hand?! (41)
فِى سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ (٤٢)
Amid infernal miasma and boiling water (42)
وَظِلٍّ مِّن يَحْمُومٍ (٤٣)
and the shadow of a dense black smoke, (43)
لَّا بَارِدٍ وَلَا كَرِيمٍ (٤٤)
neither cool nor beneficial. (44)
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ (٤٥)
Indeed they had been affluent before this, (45)
وَكَانُوا۟ يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى ٱلْحِنثِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٤٦)
and they used to persist in the great sin. (46)
وَكَانُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ أَئِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (٤٧)
And they used to say, ‘What! When we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?! (47)
أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٤٨)
And our forefathers too?!’ (48)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘Indeed the former and latter generations (49)
لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلَىٰ مِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٥٠)
will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day. (50)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبُونَ (٥١)
Then indeed, you, astray deniers, (51)
لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِن شَجَرٍ مِّن زَقُّومٍ (٥٢)
will surely eat from the Zaqqum tree (52)
فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ (٥٣)
and stuff your bellies with it, (53)
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٥٤)
and drink boiling water on top of it, (54)
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ شُرْبَ ٱلْهِيمِ (٥٥)
drinking like thirsty camels.’ (55)
هَـٰذَا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (٥٦)
Such will be the hospitality they receive on the Day of Retribution. (56)

(Commentary)

The verses detail the final destination of each of the three groups (الأزواج الثلاثة) on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: «أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ» — “They are the ones brought near [to Allah], in the Gardens of Bliss” (56:11-12). The demonstrative pronoun "those" (أُولئِكَ) refers to "the foremost" (السابقون). The phrase "Those are the ones brought near" (أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ) consists of a subject and a predicate, and the sentence is an introductory one (استئنافية). It has also been said that it is the predicate for His saying: "And the foremost" (وَالسَّابِقُونَ), and it has been said that it is a subject whose predicate is "in the Gardens of Bliss" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ). The first of these three views is the most appropriate considering the context of dividing people into three groups first, and then detailing the final outcome of each.

Nearness (القرب) and farness (البعد) are two relative concepts (معنيان متضائفان) that characterize bodies according to spatial relation (النسبة المكانية). Later, their usage was expanded to include non-spatial matters such as time and the like; for instance, it is said: "Tomorrow is near to today," "Four is nearer to three than five," and "Green is nearer to black than white." Subsequently, their usage was further expanded to include realities beyond bodies and corporeal things (الجسمانيات).

Nearness has been considered an attribute of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of His encompassing (الإحاطة) of all things. He, the Exalted, said: وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ — “When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost” (2:186); and He said: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْكُمْ — “and We are nearer to him than you” (56:85); and He said: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ — “and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein” (50:16). This meaning—namely, that He, the Exalted, is nearer to a thing than the thing is to itself—is the most wondrous conception of nearness imaginable, and we have alluded to its conceptualization in the commentary on that verse.

Nearness is also considered an attribute of the servants in the stage of servitude (العبودية). Since it is an acquired matter (اكتسابيا), the term "seeking nearness" (التقرب) is used; thus, the servant seeks nearness to Allah, the Glorified, through righteous deeds (صالح العمل), which means placing oneself in the path of divine mercy by removing the causes of wretchedness and deprivation. Allah, the Glorified, brings the servant near in the sense of granting him a station where he is uniquely entitled to receive what others do not—namely, His honor, forgiveness, and mercy. He, the Exalted, said: كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — “A written book, witnessed by those brought near” (83:20-21); and He said: وَمِزاجُهُ مِنْ تَسْنِيمٍ عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — “and its mixture is of Tasnim, a spring where those brought near drink” (83:27-28).

Thus, those brought near (المقربون) are the highest echelon of the people of felicity, as indicated by His saying: "And the foremost, the foremost; those are the ones brought near." This cannot be achieved except through the perfection of servitude (العبودية), as He said: لَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَنْ يَكُونَ عَبْداً لِلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — “The Messiah would never disdain being a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near” (4:172). Servitude is not perfected unless the servant is purely subordinate in his will and action to his Master, neither willing nor acting except as He wills. This is the entry into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah; these are the friends (أولياء) of Allah.

His saying: "In the Gardens of Bliss" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ) means that every one of them is in a Garden of Bliss, so collectively they are in the Gardens of Bliss. It is also possible that it means each of them is in multiple Gardens of Bliss, but this is made unlikely by His saying at the end of the Surah: فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ — “Then, if he be of those brought near, there shall be ease and fragrance and a garden of bliss” (56:88-89).

It has been mentioned more than once that bliss (النعيم) is the guardianship (الولاية) — the divine authority and intimacy between Allah and His servant — and that the Garden of Bliss is the Garden of Guardianship, which is consistent with what was just mentioned: that those brought near are the people of Allah's guardianship.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَقَلِيلٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ» — “A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones” (56:13-14). The large group (الثلة) is, as has been said, a numerous assembly. What is meant by "the former peoples" (الأولين) are the past nations of the previous prophets, and by "the later ones" (الآخرين) is this Ummah, as is customary in His speech whenever He mentions the former and the later together. Among such instances is what will follow in His saying: أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ أَوَآباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ — “‘Shall we really be resurrected? And our forefathers of old?’ Say, ‘Indeed the former and the latter [generations] will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day’” (56:47-50). Thus, the meaning of the two verses is: they—meaning those brought near—are a numerous group from the past nations and a few from this Ummah.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the view of some—that the "former" and "later" refer to the beginning and the end of this Ummah—is incorrect.

His saying, the Exalted: «عَلى سُرُرٍ مَوْضُونَةٍ مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَيْها مُتَقابِلِينَ» — “On gold-webbed couches, reclining on them, facing one another” (56:15-16). The term "webbed" (الوضن) refers to weaving, and it is said to mean the weaving of a coat of mail; its application to the weaving of couches is a metaphor (استعارة) intended to describe the firmness and intricacy of their weave.

His saying: "Reclining on them" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَيْها) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun referring to those brought near, and the pronoun "them" refers to the couches. His saying: "Facing one another" (مُتَقابِلِينَ) is another circumstantial qualifier for them, or for the pronoun in "reclining." Their facing one another is a metaphor for the height of their intimacy, the excellence of their social interaction, and the purity of their inner selves; they do not look at the back of their companion, nor do they find fault with him or backbite him.

The meaning is: they—the ones brought near—are settled upon woven couches while reclining on them and facing one another.

His saying, the Exalted: «يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ» — “They will be waited upon by immortal youths” (56:17). Youths (الولدان) is the plural of child (ولد), meaning a young boy. Their "circling" (طواف) around them is a metaphor for serving them. The "immortal" (المخلدون) is from immortality (الخلود) meaning permanence; i.e., they remain forever in their state of youthful freshness. It has also been said it is from al-khuld (الخلد) with two fathahs, meaning an earring, implying they are adorned with earrings.

His saying, the Exalted: «بِأَكْوابٍ وَأَبارِيقَ وَكَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ» — “with goblets, and ewers, and a cup from a flowing spring” (56:18). Goblets (الأكواب) is the plural of kub, which is a vessel without a handle or a spout. Ewers (الأباريق) is the plural of ibriq, which is a vessel that has a spout, and it is said to have both a handle and a spout. The cup (الكأس) is well-known; it is said the cup is mentioned in the singular because it is not called a ka's unless it is full. What is meant by "flowing spring" (المعين) is flowing wine that is clear to the sight and running.

His saying, the Exalted: «لا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْها وَلا يُنْزِفُونَ» — “they will not cause them any headache, nor will they be intoxicated” (56:19). That is, they are not seized by a headache due to a hangover resulting from the wine, as happens with the wine of the world, nor does their intellect vanish through the intoxication resulting from it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَفاكِهَةٍ مِمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ وَلَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ» — “and such fruits as they may choose, and such flesh of fowls as they may desire” (56:20-21). The fruits and the fowl are coordinated (معطوفان) with His saying: "with goblets." The meaning is: the youths circle around them with fruits of their choosing and with the flesh of birds they desire.

One should not find a problem with what has been mentioned in the narrations—that when the people of Paradise desire a fruit, the branch of its tree bends down to them with its fruit so they may take it, and when they desire the flesh of a bird, it falls into their hands fried or roasted, they eat what they wish from it, and then it returns to life and flies away.

This is because they have whatever they wish, and among the varieties of enjoyment (التنعم) is receiving what they desire from the hands of their servants, especially during their gatherings and celebrations, just as another variety is taking it themselves without the mediation of servants.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَحُورٌ عِينٌ كَأَمْثالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ» — “and [there will be] wide-eyed houris, like preserved pearls” (56:22-23). This is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate is omitted as indicated by the context; the estimation (التقدير) is: "and for them are wide-eyed houris" or "and therein are wide-eyed houris." The wide-eyed houris (الحور العين) are the women of Paradise, and the meaning of this term has already been explained in the commentary on Surah al-Dukhan.

His saying: "Like preserved pearls" (كَأَمْثالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ) means pearls that are protected and stored in shells, untouched by hands, thus reaching the ultimate degree of purity.

His saying, the Exalted: «جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ» — “as a reward for what they used to do” (56:24). This is a restriction for all that preceded and functions as an object of purpose (مفعول له). The meaning is: We have done for them what We did so that it may be a reward for them in exchange for the righteous deeds they used to perform consistently.

His saying, the Exalted: «لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً» — “They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech” (56:25). Vain talk (اللغو) is speech that has no benefit or resulting effect. Sinful speech (التأثيم) is the attribution of sin; i.e., none of them addresses his companion with something useless, nor does he attribute sin to him, for there is no sin there. Some have interpreted ta'thim as lying.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً» — “but only the words, ‘Peace! Peace!’” (56:26). This is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع) from vain talk and sinful speech. The word qilan (قيلاً) is a verbal noun like qawl (speech). "Peace" (سَلاماً) is an explanation for His saying "words" (قيلاً), and its repetition indicates the recurrence of its occurrence. The meaning is: except for speech that is "Peace" after "Peace."

It has been said that "Peace" (سَلاماً) could be a verbal noun acting as an adjective for "words" (قيلاً), meaning: except for speech that is peaceful (سالم).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ ما أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ» — “And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?” (56:27). This begins the detail of the final state of the "People of the Right Hand" (أصحاب الميمنة). By substituting it with "People of the Right" (أصحاب اليمين), it is known that the "People of the Right Hand" and the "People of the Right" are one and the same—they are those who are given their book in their right hand. The sentence is interrogative, intended to magnify their status and express wonder at their condition; it is the predicate for His saying: "And the People of the Right."

His saying, the Exalted: «فِي سِدْرٍ مَخْضُودٍ» — “Amidst thornless lote trees” (56:28). The lote tree (السدر) is the nabq tree. "Thornless" (المخضود) is that whose thorns have been cut off so it has no thorns.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَطَلْحٍ مَنْضُودٍ» — “and clustered acacias” (56:29). The acacia (الطلخ) is the banana tree; it is also said it is not the banana but a tree with a cool, moist shade. Others say it is the Umm Ghaylan tree which has fragrant blossoms. "Clustered" (نضد) means things placed one upon another. The meaning is: amidst banana trees with fruit clustered one upon another from bottom to top.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَظِلٍّ مَمْدُودٍ وَماءٍ مَسْكُوبٍ» — “and extended shade, and ever-flowing water” (56:30-31). It is said that the "extended" (الممدود) shade is the permanent one that is not abrogated by the sun, so it remains and does not vanish. The "ever-flowing" (المسكوب) water is that which is poured and running without interruption.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَفاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ لا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَلا مَمْنُوعَةٍ» — “and abundant fruits, neither intercepted, nor forbidden” (56:32-33). That is, not cut off (لا مَقْطُوعَةٍ) in certain seasons like the cessation of fruits in winter and the like in the world; nor forbidden (لا مَمْنُوعَةٍ) from being taken due to an internal obstacle like boredom or satiety, or an external one like distance of location, thorns preventing picking, or anything else.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَفُرُشٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ» — “and uplifted couches” (56:34). Couches (الفرش) is the plural of firash, which is a carpet or bedding. "Uplifted" (المرفوعة) means high. It has also been said that "uplifted couches" refers to women who are elevated in status regarding their intellect, beauty, and perfection, as a woman is sometimes called a firash. This meaning is consistent with His saying afterward: "Indeed, We have created them with a [special] creation..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّا أَنْشَأْناهُنَّ إِنْشاءً فَجَعَلْناهُنَّ أَبْكاراً عُرُباً أَتْراباً» — “Indeed We have created them with a [special] creation, and made them virgins, loving, of equal age” (56:35-37). That is, We have brought them into existence, originated them, and nurtured them with a special origination and nurturing. This alludes to the fact that their state does not change with youth or old age, nor in the radiance of their appearance or otherwise. His saying: "and made them virgins" (فَجَعَلْناهُنَّ أَبْكاراً) means We created them as virgins; whenever their husbands come to them, they find them virgins.

His saying: "loving, of equal age" (عُرُباً أَتْراباً). 'Urub (العرب) is the plural of 'arub, which is the woman who is affectionate toward her husband, or coquettish, or deeply in love with her husband. Atrab (الأتراب) is the plural of tirb (with kasrah then sukun), meaning a peer; i.e., they are peers to one another or peers in age to their husbands.

His saying, the Exalted: «لِأَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ» — “for the People of the Right Hand: a large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones” (56:38-40). Its meaning becomes clear from what has preceded. It is inferred from the verses that the People of the Right Hand among the later ones are a numerous group just like the former ones, but the foremost ones brought near (السابقين المقربين) among the later ones are fewer in number than those among the former ones.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ما أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ» — “And the People of the Left Hand—what are the People of the Left Hand?” (56:41). A subject and a predicate; the interrogation is for expressing wonder and terror. The "People of the Ill-omened" (أصحاب المشأمة) has been substituted with "People of the Left Hand" (أصحاب الشمال) to indicate that they are those who are given their book in their left hand, as seen in the parallel case of the People of the Right.

His saying, the Exalted: «فِي سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ وَظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ لا بارِدٍ وَلا كَرِيمٍ» — “amidst scorching winds and scalding water, and the shadow of a pitch-black smoke, neither cool nor pleasant” (56:42-44). Scorching wind (السموم)—according to al-Kashshaf—is the heat of fire that penetrates the pores. Scalding water (الحميم) is water of intense heat. The nunation (تنوين) in both is for magnifying the matter. Pitch-black smoke (اليحموم) is black smoke. His saying: "neither cool nor pleasant" (لا بارِدٍ وَلا كَرِيمٍ) apparently describes the "shadow" (الظل) and not the "pitch-black smoke," because a shadow is what is expected to provide cooling by seeking shade and rest within it, unlike smoke.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ» — “Indeed they were affluent before this” (56:45). This is a justification for the settlement of the People of the Left in punishment. The "this" refers to the mentioned punishment on the Day of Resurrection. Affluence (إتراف) of a blessing makes a person ungrateful and tyrannical; it occupies his soul such that he becomes heedless of what lies beyond it. Thus, a person being "affluent" (مترف) means his attachment to the worldly blessings he possesses and what he seeks from them, whether they be many or few.

Therefore, the objection that many of the People of the Left were not "affluent" in the sense of having expansive luxury is not valid. This is because man is surrounded by the blessings of his Lord, and a blessing is not merely wealth. Thus, his preoccupation with the blessings of his Lord away from his Lord is a form of "affluence" (ترفه) on his part. The meaning is: We only punish them with what was mentioned because they were previously in the world ungrateful and tyrannical due to the blessings.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكانُوا يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى الْحِنْثِ الْعَظِيمِ» — “and they used to persist in the great sin” (56:46). In al-Majma': al-Hinth (الحنث) is the breaking of a covenant confirmed by an oath, and persistence (الإصرار) is to remain upon it without desisting. End quote. Perhaps what is understood from the context is that their persistence in the "great sin" is their arrogance against the servitude (عبودية) to their Lord, which they had covenanted with Allah upon according to their primordial nature (الفطرة) and for which the pledge was taken from them in the world of particles (عالم الذر); thus they obey other than their Lord, which is absolute polytheism (الشرك المطلق).

It has been said that al-hinth is the great sin, so its description as "great" is for emphasis, and the "great sin" is polytheism (الشرك) with Allah. It has also been said that the "great sin" refers to the category of major sins. Others say it is the oath to deny the Resurrection, alluded to in His saying: وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لا يَبْعَثُ اللهُ مَنْ يَمُوتُ — “They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths: ‘Allah will not resurrect those who die!’” (16:38). However, the wording of the verse is general.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكانُوا يَقُولُونَ أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ أَوَآباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ» — “and they used to say, ‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we really be resurrected? And our forefathers of old?’” (56:47-48). This is a statement of theirs based on deeming it improbable (الاستبعاد); hence they emphasized the improbability of their own resurrection with the resurrection of their forefathers, because the improbability in their case is even more emphatic. The estimation is: "Shall our forefathers of old also be resurrected?"

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ» — “Say, ‘Indeed the former and the latter [generations] will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day’” (56:49-50). This is a command from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to respond to their deeming the Resurrection improbable by affirming it, and then informing them of what they will live on during the Day of Resurrection in terms of food and drink—namely, Zaqqum and Hamim.

The gist of the statement is that the former and the latter—without distinction between them, unlike how they distinguished by making their own resurrection improbable and the resurrection of their forefathers even more improbable and emphatic—will all be gathered and assembled for the tryst of a known day.

The tryst (الميقات) is that by which a thing is timed, which is its appointed time. What is meant by a "known day" is the Day of Resurrection, which is known to Allah; thus, the addition of "tryst" to "known day" is explanatory (بيانية).

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ لَآكِلُونَ مِنْ شَجَرٍ مِنْ زَقُّومٍ فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ» — “Then indeed you, O astray deniers, will surely eat from the Zaqqum tree, and fill your bellies with it” (56:51-53). This is part of the completion of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) speech, informing them of what their state will end in on the Day of Resurrection and what they will live on in terms of food and drink.

In addressing them as "astray deniers" (الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ), there is an allusion to the basis of their wretchedness and loss on the Day of Resurrection, which is their straying from the path of truth and the settling of that in their souls through their persistence in denial and their insistence on the "great sin." Had they been merely astray without denial, there would have been hope for them to be saved and not perish.

The "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from a tree" (مِنْ شَجَرٍ) is for beginning (للابتداء), and in His saying "of Zaqqum" (مِنْ زَقُّومٍ) it is explanatory (بيانية); it is also possible that "of Zaqqum" is a substitute (بدل) for "from a tree." The pronoun "it" (مِنْهَا) refers to the tree or the fruit, and both can be treated as feminine or masculine; hence the feminine pronoun is used here, while in the following verse in His saying "drinking upon it" (فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ), the masculine pronoun is used. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْحَمِيمِ فَشارِبُونَ شُرْبَ الْهِيمِ» — “and drink scalding water on top of it, drinking like thirsty camels” (56:54-55). The word "upon" (على) denotes superiority (للاستعلاء) and in this context implies that the drinking follows the eating without delay. Al-Him (الهيم) is the plural of hayma', referring to camels afflicted with huyam (with a dammah on the ha), which is a disease similar to dropsy that affects camels, causing them to drink water until they die or become severely ill. It is also said that al-him refers to sands that are never satiated with water.

The meaning is: then drinking, following what you have eaten of the Zaqqum, of the intensely hot water, drinking like the drinking of thirsty camels or like the drinking of thirsty sands. This is the end of what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to say to them.

His saying, the Exalted: «هذا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ الدِّينِ» — “This will be their hospitality on the Day of Retribution” (56:56). That is, the Day of Recompense. Hospitality (النزل) is what is presented to a descending guest in terms of food and drink as an honor to him. The meaning is: this which has been mentioned of their food and drink is the "hospitality" of the astray deniers; thus, naming what has been prepared for them as "hospitality" is a form of sarcasm (تهكم). The verse is from the speech of Him, the Exalted, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); had it been from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addressing them, it would have said: "This is your hospitality."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn 'Asakir narrated via 'Urwah ibn Ruwaym from Jabir ibn 'Abdullah who said: "When 'When the Imminent Event befalls' was revealed, and therein was mentioned: 'A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones', 'Umar said: 'O Messenger of Allah, a large group from the former and a few from the later?' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Come and listen to what Allah has revealed—“A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones”.'

Behold, from Adam to me is a large group, and my Ummah is a large group—and we shall not complete our group until we seek help from the blacks, the tenders of camels—from those who bear witness that there is no god but Allah, alone without partner." Al-Suyuti said: "Ibn Abi Hatim narrated it from another way from 'Urwah ibn Ruwaym in a disconnected (مرسلا) form." And therein: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "When 'A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones' was revealed, the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were saddened and said: 'Then there will be only a few from the Ummah of Muhammad.' So at midday there was revealed: 'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones'; you will equal the people, so the verse 'and a few from the later ones' was abrogated."

I say: In al-Kashshaf, regarding the commentary on the verse, it says: "If you say: It has been narrated that when it was revealed, it was hard upon the Muslims, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) kept appealing to his Lord until 'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones' was revealed.

I say: This is not correct for two reasons: First, this verse [the first one] clearly refers to the foremost (السابقين), and likewise the second refers to the People of the Right (أصحاب اليمين); do you not see how the People of the Right and their promise were coordinated with the foremost and their promise? Second, abrogation (النسخ) in reports (الأخبار) is not permissible." End quote.

It has been answered that the hadith can be interpreted to mean that when the companions heard the first verse, they thought that the matter in this Ummah would follow this course—that the People of the Right would be a large group from the former and a few from them—so the successful ones in Paradise from this Ummah would be fewer than those from previous nations. Then 'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones' was revealed, and their sadness vanished. The meaning of "abrogating" the previous verse would be the removal of their aforementioned thought.

However, you are well aware that this is an interpretation for which there is no evidence from the wording, and the wording rejects it—especially interpreting the abrogation of the verse as the removal of a thought. The state of the first narration, especially regarding its ending, is like the state of this narration.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They will be waited upon by immortal youths," there was a difference of opinion regarding these youths. It was said: they are the children of the people of the world who had no righteous deeds to be rewarded for, nor sins to be punished for, so they were placed in this station.

He said: It has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he was asked about the children of the polytheists? He said: "They are the servants of the people of Paradise."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Hasan, but the narration is weak and cannot be relied upon.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in Sifat al-Jannah, as did al-Bazzar, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th from 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to me: 'Indeed, you will look at a bird in Paradise—and desire it—and it will fall before you roasted.'"

I say: There are many narrations with this meaning, and in some of them, the believer eats what he desires, then the remainder returns to what it was, comes to life, flies to its place, and boasts of that.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech," he said: "Obscenity, lying, and singing (الغنا)."

I say: Perhaps what is meant by singing is that which is vain talk, or al-ghina (singing) is a scribal error for al-khana (obscenity).

And therein: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?" he said: "'Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) and his companions and his followers (شيعته)."

I say: The narration is based on what was mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ — “The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand...” (17:71); that the "Right" (اليمين) is the rightful Imam, and its meaning is that the "Right" is 'Ali (عليه السلام) and the "People of the Right" are his followers. The narration is of the type of application (الجري).

And therein: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Amidst thornless lote trees," he said: "A tree that has no leaves and no thorns in it." And Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام) read: "and clustered fruit (وطلع منضود)" and said: "one upon another."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, as did al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th from Abu Umamah who said: "The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: 'Indeed, Allah benefits us through the Bedouins and their questions.' A Bedouin came one day and said: 'O Messenger of Allah—Allah has mentioned in the Qur'an a harmful tree. I did not think that in Paradise there would be a tree that harms its owner.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'And what is it?' He said: 'The lote tree, for it has thorns.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Does not Allah say: “Amidst thornless lote trees”? Allah removes its thorns—and places in the stead of every thorn a fruit—it grows fruit that opens up—into seventy-two colors of food—no color resembles the other.'"

In al-Majma': It was narrated by the general public from 'Ali (عليه السلام): that a man read in his presence: "and clustered acacias (وَطَلْحٍ مَنْضُودٍ)," so he said: "What is the matter with the acacia? It is only 'clustered fruit' (وطلع) as in His saying: 'and palm trees with compact clusters'." It was said to him: "Will you not change it?" He said: "The Qur'an is not to be disturbed today nor moved." It was narrated from him by his son al-Hasan (عليه السلام) and Qays ibn Sa'd.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, 'Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Hannad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib: regarding His saying: "and clustered acacias," he said: "It is the banana."

In al-Majma': It was mentioned in a report: "Indeed, in Paradise there is a tree under whose shade a rider travels for a hundred years—without crossing it; read if you wish: 'and extended shade'." It was also narrated: "The times of Paradise are like the mornings of summer—there is neither heat nor cold in them."

I say: The first was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Sa'id, Anas, and others from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain from 'Ali ibn Ibrahim from Ibn Mahbub from Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Madani from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): in a hadith describing Paradise and its people: "And they visit one another and enjoy themselves in their gardens—in an extended shade like that between the break of dawn—to the rising of the sun, and more pleasant than that."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying: "Indeed We have created them with a [special] creation," he said: "The wide-eyed houris in Paradise; 'and made them virgins, loving (عُرُباً)'—he said: they do not speak except in Arabic."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: regarding His saying: 'loving (عُرُباً)'—he said: 'Their speech is Arabic.'"

I say: There are other narrations stating that 'uruban is the plural of 'arub, which is the coquettish woman.

Therein: Musaddad narrated in his Musnad, as did Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh with a good (حسن) chain from Abu Bakrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): regarding His saying, the Exalted: "A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones," he said: "They are both from this Ummah."

I say: This meaning is narrated in more than one narration, but the apparent meaning of the verses of the Surah is that the division is for all of humanity, not for this Ummah specifically. Perhaps what is meant by these narrations is the explanation of some instances (مصاديق), even if it is remote. Likewise is the meaning of what has been narrated that the People of the Right are the companions of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), and what has been narrated that the People of the Left are the enemies of the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه السلام).

In al-Mahasin, with its chain from Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام) who said: "I asked him about drinking in one breath, and he disliked it—and said: 'That is the drinking of the him (الهيم).' I said: 'And what are the him?' He said: 'Camels.'"

And therein, with its chain from al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام): that he used to dislike resembling the him. I said: "And what are the him?" He said: "The sand."

I say: Both meanings are mentioned in other narrations.

Ayahs

نَحْنُ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ فَلَوْلَا تُصَدِّقُونَ (٥٧)
We created you. Then why do you not acknowledge it? (57)
أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تُمْنُونَ (٥٨)
Have you considered the sperm that you emit? (58)
ءَأَنتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُونَ (٥٩)
Is it you who create it, or are We the creator? (59)
نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنَا بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٦٠)
We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outmaneuvered (60)
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ أَمْثَـٰلَكُمْ وَنُنشِئَكُمْ فِى مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
from replacing you with your likes and recreating you in [a realm] you do not know. (61)
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition? (62)
أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَحْرُثُونَ (٦٣)
Have you considered what you sow? (63)
ءَأَنتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلزَّٰرِعُونَ (٦٤)
Is it you who make it grow, or are We the grower? (64)
لَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ حُطَـٰمًا فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ (٦٥)
If We wish, We turn it into chaff, whereat you are left stunned [saying to yourselves,] (65)
إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ (٦٦)
‘Indeed we have suffered loss! (66)
بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ (٦٧)
No, we are deprived!’ (67)
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلْمَآءَ ٱلَّذِى تَشْرَبُونَ (٦٨)
Have you considered the water that you drink? (68)
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُزْنِ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنزِلُونَ (٦٩)
Is it you who bring it down from the rain cloud, or is it We who bring [it] down? (69)
لَوْ نَشَآءُ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ أُجَاجًا فَلَوْلَا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
If We wish We can make it bitter. Then why do you not give thanks? (70)
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى تُورُونَ (٧١)
Have you considered the fire that you kindle? (71)
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنشَأْتُمْ شَجَرَتَهَآ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنشِـُٔونَ (٧٢)
Was it you who caused its tree to grow, or were We the grower? (72)
نَحْنُ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا تَذْكِرَةً وَمَتَـٰعًا لِّلْمُقْوِينَ (٧٣)
It was We who made it a reminder and a boon for the desert-dwellers. (73)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٤)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (74)
۞ فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَٰقِعِ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٧٥)
I swear by the places where the stars set! (75)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَقَسَمٌ لَّوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ (٧٦)
And indeed it is a great oath, should you know. (76)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقُرْءَانٌ كَرِيمٌ (٧٧)
This is indeed a noble Quran, (77)
فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (٧٨)
in a guarded Book (78)
لَّا يَمَسُّهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُطَهَّرُونَ (٧٩)
—no one touches it except the pure ones— (79)
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
sent down gradually from the Lord of all the worlds. (80)
أَفَبِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَنتُم مُّدْهِنُونَ (٨١)
What! Do you take lightly this discourse? (81)
وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٨٢)
And make your denial of it your vocation? (82)
فَلَوْلَآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلْحُلْقُومَ (٨٣)
So when it reaches the throat [of the dying person], (83)
وَأَنتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنظُرُونَ (٨٤)
and at that moment you are looking on [at his bedside] (84)
وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُبْصِرُونَ (٨٥)
—and We are nearer to him than you are, though you do not perceive— (85)
فَلَوْلَآ إِن كُنتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ (٨٦)
then why do you not restore it, if you are not subject (86)
تَرْجِعُونَهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٨٧)
[to Divine dispensation], if you are truthful? (87)
فَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٨٨)
Then, if he be of those brought near, (88)
فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحَانٌ وَجَنَّتُ نَعِيمٍ (٨٩)
then ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss. (89)
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٠)
And if he be of the People of the Right Hand, (90)
فَسَلَـٰمٌ لَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩١)
then [he will be told,] ‘Peace be on you, from the People of the Right Hand!’ (91)
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٩٢)
But if he be of the impugners, the astray ones, (92)
فَنُزُلٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ (٩٣)
then a treat of boiling water (93)
وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ (٩٤)
and entry into hell. (94)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ حَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٩٥)
Indeed this is certain truth. (95)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٩٦)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme! (96)

(Commentary)

After the Glorified has detailed the final state of each of the three groups, He elaborated on the state of the "companions of the left" (أصحاب الشمال) and explained that what led them to that end was their breaking of the covenant of servitude (العبودية) and their denial of the Resurrection (البعث) and Retribution (الجزاء). He then commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to respond to them by affirming the Resurrection and Retribution and clarifying what they will be recompensed with on the Day of Resurrection.

He rebuked them for their denial of the Return (المعاد) — the restoration of life after death for judgment — despite the fact that the One informing them of it is their Creator, who manages their affairs and ordains for them death and then the second creation (الإنشاء). Thus, He knows what befalls them throughout their existence and what their final state will be. Furthermore, the Book that informs them of the Return is a Noble Quran (قرآن كريم), protected from being tampered with by the hands of the devils and their misleading allies.

Then, the discourse returns to what it began with regarding the state of the three groups, mentioning that the divergence in the conditions of these categories begins from the moment of death, and with this, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ — "We created you. Then why do you not accept the truth?" (56:57). The context is the discourse regarding the Resurrection and Retribution, which they had denied and rejected. Thus, His saying, فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ ("Then why do you not accept the truth?"), is an exhortation to believe in the account of the Return and to cease denying it. He justified this with His saying, نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ ("We created you"), as inferred from the causal derivation in His saying, فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ.

The fact that His creation of them necessitates the obligation of believing Him regarding what He informs of the Return is established from two aspects: First, that He, the Exalted, created them the first time, so He is capable of recreating them a second time, as He said: قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ — "He says, ‘Who will revive the bones when they have decayed?’ Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time, and He has knowledge of all creation.’" (36:78-79).

Second, since He, the Exalted, is their Creator and the Manager of their affairs, who ordains the specifics of their creation and their command, He is most knowledgeable of what He will do with them and what will befall them. Therefore, when He informs them that He will resurrect them after their death and recompense them for what they have done, whether good or evil, there is no choice but to believe Him. There is no excuse for one who denies what His Book has informed regarding the Resurrection and Retribution. The Exalted said: أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ — "Does He who created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware." (67:14). And He said: كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ — "As We originated the first creation, We will bring it back—a promise [binding] on Us; We will indeed do it." (21:104). And He said: وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللهِ قِيلاً — "Allah’s promise is true, and who is more truthful in speech than Allah?" (4:122).

The summary of the verse is: We created you and We know what We have done and what We shall do with you; thus, We inform you that We shall resurrect you and recompense you for what you have done—so why do you not believe what We inform you of in the Book We have sent down?

In this verse and those following it, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the second person (address), because the context is one of rebuke and censure, and that is more impactful and emphatic through direct address.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تُمْنُونَ — "Have you considered the sperm that you emit?" (56:58). Emission (الأمناء) is the ejection and pouring of semen, and what is meant is its ejection into the wombs. The meaning is: Have you considered the semen that you pour into the wombs of women?

His saying, the Exalted: أَأَنْتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الْخالِقُونَ — "Is it you who create it, or are We the creators?" (56:59). That is, do you create it into a human being like yourselves, or are We the creators of it as a human being?

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ — "We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outrun," (56:60). The management of the affairs of creation in all its aspects and specifics is a necessary concomitant of creation in the sense of the emanation of existence (إفاضة الوجود). Thus, the existence of a human being, bounded from the beginning of his formation to the last moment of his worldly life, with all the specifics that transform within him, is by the ordainment (تقدير) of his Creator, Mighty and Majestic. Therefore, his death, like his life, is by an ordainment from Him. Death does not befall him due to a deficiency in the power of his Creator to create him such that death does not befall him, nor due to causes and factors that affect him with death and thereby nullify the life that his Creator, the Exalted, emanated upon him. For the necessary consequence of that would be that His power, the Exalted, is limited and deficient, and that some causes could frustrate Him and their will could overcome His will—which is impossible. How could it be so, when Power is absolute and the Will is never overcome?

It becomes clear from this that the meaning of His saying, نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ ("We have ordained death among you"), is that death is a decreed truth and not something necessitated or required by the mode of existence of the living being; rather, He, the Exalted, ordained for him such an existence and then a death to follow it.

And the meaning of His saying, وَما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ ("and We are not to be outrun"), where outrunning (السبق) means overcoming and the one outrun (المسبوق) is the one overcome—is: We are not overcome in the occurrence of death by the causes associated with it, such that We emanation life upon you intending for it to endure, but then the causes outrun Us and overcome Us, nullifying the life whose endurance We had intended.

His saying, the Exalted: عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "that We may replace you with your likes and produce you in [a state] you do not know." (56:61). The preposition عَلى ("on/upon") is connected to His saying, قَدَّرْنا ("We ordained"), and the prepositional phrase is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال); that is: We ordained death among you while it is based upon the replacement of likes and the production in that which you do not know.

Likes (الأمثال) is the plural of mithl (مثل), which refers to that which is identical to a thing in its kind, like one human individual in relation to another. The meaning of His saying, أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ ("that We may replace your likes"), is that We replace your likes from among humanity with you, or We replace your likes in your stead. The meaning in any case is the replacement of one group with another and the placing of successors in the stead of predecessors.

His saying, وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ ("and produce you in [a state] you do not know"), where ما is a relative pronoun referring to the creation, and the sentence is coordinated with نُبَدِّلَ ("replace"). The implied meaning is: and upon [the basis] that We produce you and bring you into existence in another creation that you do not know, which is the otherworldly existence (الوجود الأخروي) as opposed to the transient worldly existence.

The summary of the meaning of the two verses is that death among you is only by an ordainment from Us, not due to a deficiency in Our power such that the continuation of your life is not feasible for Us, nor due to the dominance of destructive and annihilating causes that frustrate Us in preserving your life. Rather, We have ordained it among you on the basis of replacing likes—removing a people and bringing others—and producing a creation for you that suits the afterlife, beyond the perishing worldly creation. Thus, death is a transition from one abode to another and a transformation of creation into another creation, not an annihilation or non-existence.

Some have suggested that "likes" (الأمثال) in the verse is the plural of mathal (مثل) with two fathas, meaning "description" or "attribute" (الوصف). Thus, the two phrases عَلى أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ ("that We may replace") etc., and وَنُنْشِئَكُمْ ("and produce you") etc., would convey a single meaning. The sense would be: that We change your attributes and produce you in an attribute you do not recognize or know, such as your being gathered in the form of a dog or a pig or other animals after you were in the world in the form of a human. However, the previous meaning is more comprehensive and more beneficial.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ — "Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition?" (56:62). What is meant by the first genesis (النشأة الأولى) is the worldly genesis. Knowledge of it with its specifics necessitates the admission of another eternal genesis in which there is retribution. For it is known from the cosmic order that there is no futility or falsehood in existence; therefore, this transient genesis must have an enduring end. Furthermore, it is among the necessities of this order that everything be guided to the happiness of its kind, and the guidance of man requires the sending of messengers, the legislating of laws, and the directing of commands and prohibitions, and the retribution for good and evil deeds—which does not occur in this world, so it must be in another abode, which is the next genesis (النشأة الآخرة).

Moreover, they witnessed the first genesis and knew it, and they knew that the One who brought it into existence from the depths of non-existence is Allah, the Glorified. Since He was capable of it the first time, He is capable of bringing its like into existence a second time. The Exalted said: قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — "Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time’" (36:79). This is a proof of possibility (الإمكان) by which their deeming the Resurrection improbable is removed.

In short, through their knowledge of the first genesis, they attain knowledge of the premises of the proof for the possibility of the Resurrection, thereby removing the sense of improbability; for there is no improbability where there is possibility.

This—as you see—is a proof for the possibility of the resurrection of bodies (حشر الأجساد). Its summary is that the resurrected body is the like of the worldly body, and since the making and reviving of the worldly body was permissible, the making and reviving of the otherworldly body must also be permissible, because it is its like, and the ruling for likes (الأمثال) regarding what is permissible and what is not is one and the same.

Thus, it is surprising what al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf regarding the verse: "In this is a proof for the validity of analogy (القياس), as He charged them with ignorance for failing to analogize the next genesis to the first." (End of quote). This is because what is in the verse is a logical demonstrative syllogism (قياس برهاني منطقي), whereas what he seeks to prove by it is a jurisprudential analogy (قياس فقهي) which yields only conjecture (الظن)—so how far apart are the two?

And it is said in Ruh al-Ma'ani regarding the verse: "Then why do you not remember that He who was capable of it—meaning the first genesis—is even more capable of the next genesis? For it involves less 'making' due to the availability of materials, the specification of parts, and the precedence of the model (المثال). This, according to what they said, is a proof for the validity of analogy, though it was said: it only indicates the 'analogy of the a fortiori' (قياس الأولي) because that is what is in the verse." (End of quote).

In this is the same error as the previous one. Furthermore, what is in the verse is not an "analogy of the a fortiori" at all, because the common link between the first and the next genesis is that they are two likes, and the principle of the syllogism is that the ruling for likes regarding what is permissible and what is not is one.

As for his saying that the next genesis involves "less making" due to the availability of materials and the specification of parts, this is rejected. For materials require the emanation of existence for their subsistence (بقاء) just as they require it for their origination (حدوث) and their first attainment. Likewise, the specification of parts requires it for subsistence just as it requires it initially; thus, the making the second time is like the making the first time.

As for his saying, "and the precedence of the model (المثال)," he has confused the "like" (المثل) with the "model" (المثال). The otherworldly body, in relation to itself, is the like of the worldly body, not according to its model; for if it were according to its model, the Hereafter would be the world and not the Hereafter.

If you say: If the otherworldly body is a like (مثل) of the worldly body, and the like of a thing is other than it, then the human being returned in the Hereafter would be other than the human being started in the world, because he is his like, not his very self (عينه).

I say: It has been mentioned in previous discussions more than once that the identity (شخصية) of a human being is by his spirit (الروح), not by his body. The spirit is not annihilated by death; rather, the body decays and its parts scatter. Then, when it is fashioned a second time like what it was in the world and the spirit is attached to it, the human being is the very same human being who was in the world, just as the elderly Zayd is the very same as the young Zayd, due to the persistence of the spirit in its identity despite the body changing moment after moment.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَحْرُثُونَ — "Have you considered what you sow?" (56:63) until His saying مَحْرُومُونَ — "deprived." (56:67). After He reminded them of the manner of their own creation and the ordainment of death among them as a preparation for the Resurrection and Retribution—all of which are concomitants of His Lordship (ربوبيته)—He enumerated for them three matters among the most important things by which they live in the world: the crop (الزرع) which they eat, the water (الماء) which they drink, and the fire (النار) by which they warm themselves and which they use for many of their needs. Through these, His Lordship over them is established, for Lordship is nothing but management (التدبير) arising from ownership.

So He said: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَحْرُثُونَ ("Have you considered what you sow?"). Sowing (الحرث) is working the land and casting the seed upon it. أَأَنْتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُ ("Is it you who make it grow?")—that is, do you cause it to sprout and develop until it reaches its end? The pronoun in تَزْرَعُونَهُ refers to the seed or the sowing known from the context. أَمْ نَحْنُ الزَّارِعُونَ ("or are We the growers?")—the ones who cause it to sprout and develop until it is complete as a crop. لَوْ نَشاءُ لَجَعَلْناهُ حُطاماً ("If We wished, We would surely turn it into chaff")—that is, broken, crumbled, and dry. فَظَلْتُمْ ("and you would be left")—that is, you would become تَفَكَّهُونَ ("wondering")—that is, marveling at what has afflicted your crop and talking about what happened, saying: إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ ("‘We are indeed debt-ridden!’")—fallen into debt and loss; our wealth is gone, our time wasted, and our effort failed. بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ ("‘Indeed we are deprived!’")—prevented from provision and good.

There is no contradiction between denying the "growing" (الزرع) from them and attributing it to Him, the Exalted, and the mediation of natural factors and causes in the sprouting and growth of the crop. For the discussion returns to the influence and making of these causes; their mode of influence is not by an exigency of their own essence independent of Him, the Exalted, but rather by His making, placing, and gifting. The same applies to the causes of these causes, and the matter ends with Allah, the Glorified—and indeed, to your Lord is the final end.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ الْماءَ الَّذِي تَشْرَبُونَ — "Have you considered the water that you drink?" (56:68) until His saying فَلَوْ لا تَشْكُرُونَ — "Then why do you not give thanks?" (56:70). The clouds (المزن) are the rain-clouds. His saying, فَلَوْ لا تَشْكُرُونَ ("Then why do you not give thanks?"), is an exhortation to gratitude (الشكر). Gratitude to Him, the Exalted, is the beautiful mention of Him for His blessings, which is the manifestation of one's servitude (العبادة) in word and deed. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ النَّارَ الَّتِي تُورُون — "Have you considered the fire that you strike?" (56:71) until His saying وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ — "and a convenience for the desert-travelers." (56:73). It is said in al-Majma': Striking (الإيراء) is the manifestation of fire by friction; it is said awra yuri. He said: It is said "he struck and it ignited" (أورى) when it appears, and if it does not ignite, it is said "he struck and it failed" (أكبى). He said: The traveler (المقوي) is the one who alights in the desert (القواء) where there is no one; and a house is said to be aqwat when it is empty of its inhabitants. (End of quote). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ — "So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme." (56:74). This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When He, the Glorified, mentioned the evidences of His Lordship over them—that He is the one who creates them and manages their affairs, and that part of His management is that He will resurrect them and recompense them for their deeds while they are deniers of that—He turned away from addressing them and turned to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), indicating that they do not comprehend the speech. Thus, He commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to hallow Him, the Exalted, from their associating partners with Him and their denial of the Resurrection and Retribution.

So His saying, فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ ("So celebrate the Name") etc., the fa is for the derivation of the glorification (التسبيح) from the preceding explanation. The ba is for seeking assistance (الاستعانة) or accompaniment (الملابسة). The meaning is: Since it is so, then glorify while seeking assistance through the mention of the Name of your Lord. Or what is meant by the Name (الاسم) is the mention (الذكر), because the utterance of the name of a thing is a mention of it, as has been said. Or the ba is for transitivity (التعدية), because the hallowing of the name of a thing is the hallowing of the thing itself. The meaning is: Hallow the Name of your Lord from mentioning a partner for Him or denying the Resurrection and Retribution from Him. "The All-supreme" (العظيم) is an attribute of the Lord or the Name.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ — "I swear by the places where the stars set!" (56:75). فَلا أُقْسِمُ is an oath. It is said that la is extra and uqsimu is the oath. It is also said that la is negating and uqsimu is the oath.

"Places" (مواقع) is the plural of mawqi' (موقع), which is the location. The meaning is: I swear by the locations of the stars in the sky. It is also said that mawaqi' is the plural of mawqi', a mim-infinitive meaning "falling," referring to the falling of the stars on the Day of Resurrection, or the falling of meteors upon the devils, or the setting-places of the stars in the west. The first of these views is the one that first comes to mind.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ — "And it is indeed a great oath, if you only knew!" (56:76). This is an exaltation of this oath and an emphasis upon emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ — "This is indeed a noble Quran," (56:77) until His saying مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "from the Lord of all the worlds." (56:80). Since their denial of the account of His Oneness (وحدانيته) in Lordship and Divinity, as well as their denial of the Resurrection and Retribution, was manifested through their denial of the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—which contains the news of Monotheism (التوحيد) and the Resurrection—their denial branched into two: a denial of the origin of Monotheism and the Resurrection fundamentally, and a denial of that insofar as the Quran informs them of it. Therefore, He, the Exalted, first presented an explanation to establish the origin of Oneness and the Resurrection by mentioning evidences from His signs that prove that, which is His saying: نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ ("We created you") until His saying وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ ("and a convenience for the desert-travelers"). Secondly, He presented an explanation emphasizing that the Noble Quran is His speech, preserved with Him and revealed from Him, describing it with its finest attributes.

So His saying, إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ ("This is indeed a noble Quran"), is the response to the preceding oath. The pronoun refers to the Quran known from the preceding context. It is inferred from its description as "noble" (كريم) without restriction in a context of praise that it is noble to Allah and dear to Him, noble in its praised attributes, and noble in its generosity and benefit to people due to the principles of knowledge (المعارف) it contains, which lead to the happiness of this world and the Hereafter.

His saying, فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ — "in a protected Book," (56:78) is a second attribute of the Quran; that is, preserved and protected from change and substitution. It is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as He, the Exalted, said: بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ — "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet." (85:21-22).

His saying, لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ — "no one touches it except the pure ones." (56:79). This is an attribute of the "protected Book," and it is possible for it to be a third attribute of the Quran. The outcome of both views, assuming la is negating, is the same.

The meaning is: No one touches the protected Book in which the Quran is contained except the pure ones, or no one touches the Quran which is in the Book except the pure ones.

The speech, in any case, is intended to exalt the matter of the Quran and glorify it. Thus, "touching" (المس) it is the knowledge (العلم) of it while it is in the protected Book, as indicated by His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ — "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed with Us in the Mother Book, [and it is] surely high and wise." (43:3-4).

The "pure ones" (المطهرون)—the passive participle of "purification" (التطهير)—are those whose souls Allah, the Exalted, has purified from the filth of disobedience and the impurities of sins, or from that which is even greater and more subtle: the purification of their hearts from attachment to other than Him, the Exalted. This meaning of purification is what is appropriate for "touching," which is knowledge, rather than purification from physical impurity (الخبث) or ritual impurity (الحدث), as is apparent.
