Thus, the pure ones are those whom Allah, the Exalted, has honored by purifying their souls, such as the noble angels and those among humans whom Allah has purified. The Exalted said: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيراً — "Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification." (33:33). There is no reason to restrict the "pure ones" to the angels, as most commentators have done, for that would be a restriction without a restrictor.

It is sometimes suggested that la in لا يَمَسُّهُ is prohibitive (ناهية), and "touching" in this case refers to touching the script of the Quran, and "purity" refers to purity from ritual impurity, or both ritual and physical impurity. It has also been recited as al-muttahhirun (المطهرون) with a shadda on the ta and ha and a kasra on the ha, meaning "those who purify themselves." The implication of the verse would then be the prohibition of touching the script of the Quran without purity.

It is possible to carry the verse to this meaning even if la is negating, by the sentence being an informative statement intended as a command (إنشاء), which is more eloquent than a direct command.

Al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf: "If you make it—meaning the sentence لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ—an attribute of the Quran, the meaning is: It is not fitting that anyone should touch it except one who is in a state of purity among people, meaning touching the written form of it." (End of quote). You have already seen the validity of intending "touching" as knowledge and awareness, assuming it is an attribute of the Quran, just as it is valid assuming it is an attribute of the protected Book.

His saying, تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "sent down from the Lord of all the worlds." (56:80). This is another attribute of the Quran. The infinitive is in the sense of the passive participle; that is, "sent down" (منزل) from Allah to you, so that you may understand and reason it after it was in a protected Book that none touch except the pure ones.

The expression of Him, the Exalted, as "the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين) is to indicate that His Lordship, the Exalted, is spread over all the worlds, and they are among them. Thus, He, the Exalted, is their Lord, and if He is their Lord, it is incumbent upon them to believe in His Book, listen to His speech, and accept it as true without denial.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَبِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَنْتُمْ مُدْهِنُونَ — "Do you then take this discourse lightly," (56:81). The reference in "this discourse" (بهذا الحديث) is to the Quran. "Taking lightly" (الإدهان) is to be negligent or compromising regarding it. Its origin is "softening with oil" (التليين بالدهن), used metaphorically for negligence. The interrogative is for rebuke; He, the Exalted, rebukes them for considering the matter of the Quran as something trivial not to be cared for.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ — "and make your denial your [only] share [of it]?" (56:82). It is said that "provision" (الرزق) refers to their share of good. The meaning is: You make your share of the good that you could attain through the Quran to be your denial of it; that is, you put denial in its place. It is also said that "provision" refers to the Quran itself, which Allah provided for them. The meaning is: You take denial in place of this provision that you were given. It is also said that the speech has a suppressed mudaf (possessive), and the implied meaning is: "And you make the gratitude for your provision to be your denial"—that is, you have put denial in the place of gratitude.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ — "So when it reaches the throat," (56:83) until His saying صادِقِينَ — "truthful." (56:87). This is a return to the beginning of the discourse by deriving from their denial that: If you are truthful in your negation of the Resurrection and correct in your denial of this Quran which informs you of the Resurrection, then return the soul of the dying person when it has reached the throat. For if death were not by an ordainment from Allah, it would be among the accidental matters for which a stratagem might be found to repel it. Since you are unable to return it and restore life with it, then know that death is a truth ordained by Allah to drive the souls toward the Resurrection and Retribution.

So His saying, فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ, is derived from their denial of the Quran and what it informs of the Resurrection and Retribution. لو لا is for exhortation (التحضيض) to demonstrate their incapacity and to silence them. The pronoun in بَلَغَتِ ("it reaches") refers to the soul (النفس), and the soul reaching the throat is a metaphor for the complete imminence of death.

His saying, وَأَنْتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنْظُرُونَ ("and you are looking on at that moment")—that is, you are looking at the dying person; he is within your sight.

His saying, وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْكُمْ وَلكِنْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ ("and We are nearer to him than you are, but you do not perceive")—that is, while the case is that We are nearer to him than you are due to Our encompassing him in existence, and Our messengers who seize his soul are nearer to him than you are, but you do not see Us nor Our messengers.

The Exalted said: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death" (39:42). And He said: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you’" (32:11). And He said: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا — "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him" (6:61).

His saying, فَلَوْ لا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ ("then why do you not—if you are not to be judged")—the repetition of law la is to emphasize the previous law la. "Judged" (مدينين) means recompensed, from dana yadinu meaning jaza yajzi. The meaning is: If you are not to be recompensed with reward and punishment through the Resurrection.

His saying, تَرْجِعُونَها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ("restore it, if you are truthful?")—that is, if you are truthful in your claim that there is no Resurrection and no Retribution. His saying تَرْجِعُونَها ("restore it") is the object of the exhortative law la according to the implied structure. The arrangement of the verses according to the implied structure is: "Then why do you not restore it when it reaches the throat, if you are not to be judged?"

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ — "Then if he be of those brought near, then [there shall be] ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss." (56:88-89). This is a return to the explanation of the state of the three groups mentioned at the beginning of the Surah at the time of death and after it. The pronoun in كانَ ("he be") refers to the deceased, known from the context. What is meant by "those brought near" (المقربين) are the "foremost, those brought near" (السابقون المقربون) mentioned previously. "Ease" (الروح) is comfort. "Abundance" (الريحان) is provision (الرزق). It is also said that it is the fragrant basil (الريحان) from the basil of Paradise brought to him, which he smells and then passes away.

The meaning is: As for if the deceased is among those brought near, then for him—or his recompense is—comfort from every worry, grief, and pain, and provision from the provision of Paradise, and a garden of bliss.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ — "And if he be of the companions of the right, then ‘Peace be to you, from the companions of the right.’" (56:90-91). It is possible that the lam is for possessive specialization (الاختصاص الملكي), and the meaning of فَسَلامٌ لَكَ is that you are specialized with peace from the companions of the right who are your peers and companions; thus, you see nothing from them but good and peace.

It is also said that laka (to you) means 'alayka (upon you); that is, the companions of the right greet you with peace. Other things have also been said.

The transition from the third person to the second person (address) is to indicate that he is addressed with this greeting: "Peace be to you from the companions of the right."

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ الضَّالِّينَ فَنُزُلٌ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ — "But if he be of the deniers, the astray, then a hospitality of boiling water, and entry into hell." (56:92-94). "Entry" (تصلية) into the Fire is being made to enter it. It is also said: enduring its heat and punishment.

The meaning is: But if he be of the people of denial and straying, then for them is a hospitality of water of intense heat, and the enduring of the heat of the fire of Hell.

Allah has described them as "the deniers, the astray," placing denial before straying because the punishment they encounter is a consequence of their denial and their stubbornness against the truth. If it were straying without denial and stubbornness, they would be among the "oppressed" (المستضعفين) and would not occupy this station. As for His previous saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ ("Then indeed, you, O astray deniers"), since the context there was one of responding to their saying: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ ("When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?"), it was more appropriate to describe them first with straying and then with denial.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ — "Indeed this is the certain truth." (56:95). "Truth" (الحق) is knowledge insofar as the external reality corresponds to it. "Certainty" (اليقين) is knowledge in which there is no confusion or doubt. Thus, the annexation of "truth" to "certainty" is a type of explanatory annexation (إضافة بيانية) brought for emphasis.

The meaning is: Indeed, this which We have mentioned regarding the state of the three groups of people is the truth in which there is no hesitation, and the knowledge which no doubt affects.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ — "So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme." (56:96). Its interpretation has preceded. It is derived from what preceded it of the description of the Quran and the explanation of the state of the three groups after death and in the Gathering.

The meaning is: Since the Quran is of these attributes and is truthful in what it informs of the state of people after death, then hallow your All-supreme Lord while seeking assistance from or being accompanied by His Name, and negate what these astray deniers see and claim.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَأَنْتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الزَّارِعُونَ ("Is it you who make it grow, or are We the growers?"). It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: لا يقولن أحدكم زرعت وليقل حرثت — "None of you should say, 'I grew [the crop],' but let him say, 'I sowed [the crop].'"

I say: It is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur by several compilers of collections from Abu Hurayrah from him (ص).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: أَأَنْتُمْ أَنْزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ الْمُزْنِ ("Is it you who sent it down from the rain-clouds?"). He said: "From the clouds." نَحْنُ جَعَلْناها تَذْكِرَةً ("We have made it a reminder")—for the fire of the Day of Resurrection. وَمَتاعاً لِلْمُقْوِينَ ("and a convenience for the desert-travelers"). He said: "The needy."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ ("So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme"). It is authentically reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that when this verse was revealed, he said: اجعلوها في ركوعكم — "Place it in your bowing (ركوع)."

I say: It is narrated in al-Faqih as a mursal report, and narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Juhani from him (ص).

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Muhammad ibn Nasr, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "The Quran was sent down on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) from the highest heaven to the lowest heaven all at once, then it was divided over the years." In another wording: "Then it was sent down from the lowest heaven to the earth in stages (نجوما)." Then he recited: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ ("I swear by the places where the stars set!").

I say: Its apparent meaning is the interpretation of "the places where the stars set" (مواقع النجوم) as the times of the descent of the stages of the Quran.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ. He said: "Its meaning is: I swear by the places where the stars set."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a chain of transmission from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ. He said: "عند الله في صحف مطهرة — 'With Allah in purified scrolls.'" لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ. He said: "المقربون — 'Those brought near.'"

I say: The interpretation of "the pure ones" (المطهرون) as "those brought near" (المقربون) supports what we presented in the preceding commentary. We have already cited, under His saying: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ (45:29), a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the "protected Book."

In al-Majma': Regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ. They said: "It is not permissible for the one in a state of major ritual impurity (جنب), the menstruating woman (حائض), or the one in a state of minor ritual impurity (محدث) to touch the Mushaf (the written Quran)." Narrated from Imam Muhammad ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام).

I say: What is meant by "touching the Mushaf" is touching its script, as evidenced by other narrations.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Dawud ibn Farqad from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), he said: "I asked him about an amulet (التعويذ) hung upon a menstruating woman." He said: "نعم لا بأس. وقال : تقرؤه وتكتبه ولا تصيبه يدها — 'Yes, there is no harm.' And he said: 'She may read it and write it, but her hand should not touch it.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Abi Dawud, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Abd Allah ibn Abi Bakr from his father, who said: "In the letter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Amr ibn Hazm: ولا تمس القرآن إلا عن طهور — 'And do not touch the Quran except in a state of purity.'"

I say: The narrations regarding this are numerous from both the Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In it, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "It rained upon the people during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: أصبح من الناس شاكر ومنهم كافر — 'This morning, some people are grateful and some are disbelievers.' They said: 'This is a mercy Allah has placed.' But some of them said: 'Such and such a star-phase (نوء) was truthful.' Then this verse was revealed: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ until it reached وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ."

I say: The narration has become widespread through Sunni paths that the verses were revealed regarding the star-phases (الأنواء). Its apparent meaning is that they are Medinan, but this does not harmonize with the context of the verses of the Surah, as you have seen.

In al-Majma': The recitation of Ali (عليه‌السلام) and Ibn Abbas, and it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): وتجعلون شكركم ("and you make your gratitude").

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Ali (عليه‌السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ. He said: "Its meaning is: if you were not to be recompensed for your deeds." تَرْجِعُونَها ("restore it")—meaning the spirit when it reaches the throat—"you return it to the body, إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ('if you are truthful')."

In it, with its chain of transmission from Abu Basir, he said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) saying: فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ — 'in his grave,' وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ — 'in the Hereafter.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Qasim ibn Mandah narrated in Kitab al-Ahwal wa al-Iman bi al-Su'al from Salman, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'The first thing a believer is given glad tidings of at the time of death is ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss. And the first thing a believer is given glad tidings of in his grave is that it is said: "Receive glad tidings of the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted, and Paradise; you have come with the best of arrivals. Allah has forgiven those who escorted you to your grave, confirmed the one who bore witness for you, and answered the one who sought forgiveness for you."'"

In it, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ. He said: "The angels come to him with peace from Allah—they greet him and inform him that he is among the companions of the right."

I say: The meaning he presented is based on the verse being a report of the speech of the angels. The implied meaning is: "The angels said: 'Peace be to you,' while you are among the companions of the right." Thus, it is both a greeting and glad tidings.
